title: Basic OOP: A Reading That Knows How to Classify Itself
learningObjectives: Define a class that bundles related data and behavior together, rather than a dictionary plus separate functions
Explain the difference between a class and an instance
Write an __init__ method and instance methods that operate on self
Recognize when a class is a genuine improvement over a plain dictionary, and when it's unnecessary ceremony
whyItMatters: A dictionary works for one reading, but the Digital Twin needs a growing number of behaviors attached to a reading — classify itself, validate itself, report itself — and every scikit-learn/PyTorch object encountered from Mission 3 onward follows exactly this pattern: data and behavior bundled together. This is the last core Python concept needed before the codebase this mission built becomes the real seed of the AI Energy OS.
capstoneContribution: The SensorReading class — the Digital Twin's first proper object, replacing the plain dictionary used throughout this mission with something that can validate and classify itself.

problem.summary: Nothing ties a reading's data to the logic that assumes it's shaped correctly.
problem.content: Your reading dictionaries and your `classify_demand()` function are two separate things that happen to be used together everywhere. Nothing stops someone from calling `classify_demand()` on a dictionary that's missing required fields, because the dictionary has no idea what a valid reading even looks like.

motivation.summary: Every scikit-learn/PyTorch object you'll use next mission is exactly this pattern.
motivation.content: This exact pattern — a plain dict plus a pile of functions that assume it's shaped correctly — is what a class exists to fix. Every scikit-learn model and PyTorch layer you'll use starting next mission is an object exactly like the one you're about to build: data and the behavior that operates on it, bundled together.

theory.summary: A class is a blueprint; an instance is a specific object built from it; self refers to the instance's own data.
theory.content: `class SensorReading:` defines a blueprint; `SensorReading(...)` creates an instance — a specific reading built from that blueprint. `def __init__(self, ...):` runs once when an instance is created, typically to store the passed-in values as `self.demand_kw = demand_kw`. Instance methods (`def classify(self):`) take `self` as their first parameter and can read the instance's own stored data without it being passed in again. A class earns its complexity exactly when data and the behavior around it belong together and get reused as a unit — for a single throwaway calculation, a plain dictionary or bare variable is still the simpler, right choice.

visualExplanation.summary: One blueprint produces several instances, each with its own data but a shared method.
visualExplanation.content: The `SensorReading` blueprint (class) sits on one side, and three concrete reading instances built from it sit on the other, each with its own `demand_kw` value but sharing the exact same `classify()` method definition.

interactiveDemo.summary: Stamp out several instances from one blueprint and call classify() on each.
interactiveDemo.content: Fill in a class blueprint's fields, then 'stamp out' several instances with different values, calling `classify()` on each and watching each instance produce its own independent result from the shared method.

guidedCoding.summary: Follow along: define SensorReading with __init__ and a classify() method.

independentCoding.summary: Define BatteryReading with its own classify() method, unaided.
independentCoding.content: Define a `BatteryReading` class with `soc_percent` and a `classify()` method reusing the CRITICAL/LOW/OK thresholds from earlier lessons, then create three instances and print each one's classification.

debugging.summary: Fix a method definition that forgot self.

reflection.summary: Bundling behavior with data changes how confident you can be that it's used correctly.
reflection.content: Now that `classify()` lives on the reading itself, what changed about how confident you can be that it's always called on correctly-shaped data, compared to the standalone `classify_demand(reading_dict)` function from before?

teachBack.summary: Explain why bundling data and behavior together is different from keeping them separate.
teachBack.content: Explain to someone who's only used dictionaries and functions why bundling `demand_kw` and `classify()` together into one `SensorReading` class is different from just keeping them separate, using a concrete example of what goes wrong without the class.

miniProject.summary: Rewrite the rolling window as a list of SensorReading instances.
miniProject.content: Rewrite the entire sensor-intake mini project's rolling window as a list of `SensorReading` instances instead of a list of dictionaries, with classification now called as a method on each reading.

portfolioEvidence.summary: SensorReading is the Digital Twin's first real object.
portfolioEvidence.content: SensorReading is the Digital Twin's first real object — the shape every later mission's DataFrame rows, model predictions, and RL states will echo.

labs.title: SensorReading Class
labs.description: Sandboxed exercise requiring the learner to define a SensorReading class with a correct __init__ and classify() method, then use it as a drop-in replacement across the accumulated mission project.

simulations.title: Class & Instance Visualizer
simulations.description: Shows one class blueprint producing multiple independent instances, each with its own data but a shared method definition.

flashcards.front: What's the difference between SensorReading (the class) and reading (an instance of it)?
flashcards.back: SensorReading is the blueprint that defines what fields and methods every reading has. reading = SensorReading(4821, 'substation-12') is one specific object built from that blueprint, holding its own actual values.

flashcards.front: Why does an instance method need self as its first parameter?
flashcards.back: self is how the method accesses the specific instance's own stored data (e.g. self.demand_kw) rather than some other instance's — Python passes it in automatically when you call reading.classify(), but the method definition must declare it explicitly.

rubric: Correctly explains what a class and an instance are, using a concrete example rather than only the box/blueprint metaphor
Justifies, with a specific example, when a class is worth its added complexity over a plain dictionary and when it isn't
SensorReading implementation correctly bundles data and behavior, with self used correctly throughout every instance method
